title: Potted Shrimp
subtitle: Brown shrimp sealed in spiced clarified butter, served cold on hot toast
excerpt: Potted shrimp is a cold preparation: brown shrimp mixed with warm spiced butter, packed into ramekins, and sealed under a layer of clarified butter. The sealed butter preserves the shrimp and carries the mace and cayenne flavour. It takes 20 minutes to make and needs two hours to chill.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: potted shrimps entry. Potted shrimp is associated with the Morecambe Bay fisheries of Lancashire and is documented in British cookbooks from the 18th century. It is one of the few British dishes with a clear regional identity and unchanged preparation.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 3 days ahead and keep refrigerated. The butter seal preserves the shrimp.
recipe.temperatureNote: No oven required; brief hob work and then chilled.

glossaryTerms.definition: Butter from which the milk solids and water have been removed, leaving pure golden fat. Melt the butter gently and spoon off the white foam; the clear fat beneath is clarified butter. It sets solid at cool temperatures and forms an airtight seal over potted foods.

content.text: Potted shrimp is one of the most economical preparations in British cooking: a small amount of good brown shrimp, preserved in butter and spices and spread on hot toast. The butter is the flavour carrier; the mace and cayenne go in with the shrimp, and the clarified seal locks everything in. Use Morecambe Bay brown shrimp if available.

items.prepNote: 60 g for the shrimp, 90 g for the clarified seal

content.text: Melt 60 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a small saucepan over a low heat. Add the mace, cayenne, and nutmeg and stir for 30 seconds. Add the {{brown-shrimp}} of shrimp, lemon juice, salt, and white pepper. Stir gently until the shrimp are warmed through, about 1 minute. Do not let the butter brown.
Divide the shrimp and their butter between 4 small ramekins. Press down gently so the surface is level. Leave to cool.
Melt the remaining 90 g of butter in a clean saucepan over a very low heat. Skim off the white foam with a spoon to make
. Pour the clear golden fat gently over the shrimp in each ramekin, leaving the milky residue in the pan. The butter should cover the shrimp completely.
Refrigerate for at least 2 hours until the butter seal is completely firm. Serve straight from the ramekins with hot toast.

items.symptom: Butter seal is cloudy rather than clear
items.cause: Milk solids were not fully removed before pouring.
items.fix: Skim the foam carefully and pour slowly, stopping when you see the milky residue at the bottom of the pan. A cloudy seal will still taste fine but the appearance is less clean.

items.cause: Pre-cooked shrimp were cooked again for too long.
items.fix: Warm the shrimp in the butter for 30 to 60 seconds only. They are already cooked; you are just transferring the spiced butter, not cooking them again.
Taste the buttered shrimp before potting and add more mace, cayenne, or lemon juice as needed. The sealed flavour is fixed once chilled.

content.text: Potted shrimp is one of the most regionally specific dishes in this collection. Brown shrimp from the bay at Morecambe in Lancashire have been processed and potted there since the 18th century, and the traditional preparation (mace, cayenne, clarified butter seal) has not changed. The dish appears in the Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management and in earlier 18th-century household management guides. It is a preservation technique as much as a recipe: the clarified butter seal, if airtight, keeps the shrimp for several days and carries the spiced flavour into the butter. Serve it on toast that is hot enough to start melting the butter seal on contact.
